questions.prompt: What is the dispatch rule that distinguishes a protocol requirement from an extension-only method?

options.text: A method listed in the protocol body (a requirement) uses dynamic dispatch — the conformer's implementation wins even through the protocol type; a method that exists only in the extension uses static dispatch — which implementation runs is decided by the reference's compile-time type.
Both requirement and extension-only methods always use dynamic dispatch; the conformer's version always wins.
Both always use static dispatch; the extension's version always wins regardless of the conformer.
Requirements are static and extension-only methods are dynamic — the reverse of what most people assume.

questions.explanation: The chapter's central rule: requirements → dynamic (conformer wins even through the protocol type); extension-only → static (chosen by the reference's static type). (b) and (c) each collapse the distinction that is the whole point. (d) reverses the rule — requirements are the dynamic ones.

questions.prompt: Given the code, what does d.label() print, and why?

options.text: It prints 'unlabeled' — label() is not a protocol requirement, so it is statically dispatched against the Describable reference type, and Widget's own label() is not consulted at all (nothing is actually overridden; the two label() methods are independent).
It prints 'shiny widget' — the actual object is a Widget, so its method always wins.
It fails to compile because Widget redefines an extension method.
It prints 'generic' — the kind() default leaks into label().

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's exact trap: label() is extension-only, so the call resolves against the static type Describable and prints 'unlabeled' — Widget's label() is a separate, statically-dispatched method that isn't consulted through the protocol reference. (b) is the intuitive-but-wrong answer the chapter builds the whole lesson around. (c) is false; redefining an extension method compiles fine. (d) confuses label() with the unrelated kind() default.

questions.prompt: For the same object, why can d.kind() print the conformer's value while d.label() prints the extension's, when both are accessed through a Describable reference?

options.text: kind() is declared in the protocol body, so it is a requirement dispatched dynamically (conformer wins); label() exists only in the extension, so it is dispatched statically against the reference type — the difference is requirement-vs-extension, not anything about the object.
Because kind() returns a String and label() returns an optional, and optionals are dispatched differently.
Because the object is sometimes a Widget and sometimes not, depending on runtime state.
Because kind() is called first, and the first method call on an object is always dynamic.

questions.explanation: The chapter's key insight: the divergence is purely requirement (dynamic) vs extension-only (static), not a property of the object. (b) fabricates a return-type dependency. (c) is false; the same object is used throughout. (d) invents a call-order rule that doesn't exist.

questions.prompt: You want a protocol extension method to be overridable per conformer through the protocol type. What is the fix?

options.text: Declare the method as a protocol requirement (list it in the protocol body); the extension can still provide a default, but being a requirement is what moves it into dynamic dispatch so conformers' overrides win through the protocol type.
Mark the extension method with the dynamic keyword; that alone makes any extension method dynamically dispatched.
Make the conformer a class instead of a struct; extension methods are dynamic for classes.
There is no fix; extension methods can never be overridden through a protocol type.

questions.explanation: The chapter's fix: list the method as a requirement in the protocol body — that (not merely being in an extension) is what enables dynamic dispatch, while the extension still supplies a default. (b) is misleading — the requirement declaration is the mechanism here, not a bare dynamic keyword on an extension method. (c) is false; the requirement-vs-extension rule is independent of struct vs class. (d) is false; declaring it a requirement is exactly the fix.

questions.prompt: Why does Swift dispatch extension-only methods statically rather than dynamically?

options.text: Extension-only methods aren't part of the protocol's witness (dynamic dispatch) table unless declared as requirements; they are ordinary statically-dispatched functions in scope for conformers — a deliberate performance and predictability choice, even though it violates the 'the real object's method always wins' intuition.
It is an acknowledged compiler bug that the Swift team plans to change to dynamic dispatch in a future release.
Because static dispatch is required for any method that returns a value; only void methods can be dynamic.
Because extensions are compiled after the main type, so their methods lose dynamic dispatch as a side effect of compilation order.

questions.explanation: The chapter explains it as a deliberate design: extension-only methods live outside the witness table, dispatched statically for performance and predictability, at the cost of surprising the inheritance intuition. (b) is false; it's intentional, not a bug slated for change. (c) fabricates a return-type rule. (d) invents a compilation-order cause.

questions.prompt: Per the chapter's trade-off, should you make every protocol extension method a requirement to avoid the static-dispatch surprise?

options.text: No — declaring everything a requirement reintroduces ceremony and gives up static dispatch's performance for helpers that never needed overriding; the judgment is intent: customization points should be requirements, while internal conveniences built on those points are fine as extension-only.
Yes — every extension method should always be a requirement, with no exceptions, to guarantee dynamic dispatch everywhere.
No — you should never declare requirements at all and rely entirely on extensions.
It doesn't matter; requirement vs extension has no practical effect once the code compiles.

questions.explanation: The chapter's trade-off: requirement for genuine customization points, extension-only for internal helpers where static dispatch is fine and even desirable — blanket-requiring everything is needless ceremony and lost performance. (b) is the overcorrection the trade-off argues against. (c) overcorrects the other way. (d) contradicts the entire chapter, which shows the effect is a real, bug-causing behavioral difference.
